The integration of structural modeling with patient-derived network analysis will identify cooperative-binding-dependent gene programs relevant for prognosis and therapy.
proposals.novelty: While p53-DNA binding is well-studied, explicitly testing for *direct* DBD-DBD cooperation versus indirect mechanisms using integrated structural and network approaches is novel. Applying geometric network analysis to connect atomic-level structural predictions of protein domain cooperation with patient-level transcriptomic dysregulation represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Population-based graph genome analysis reveals genetic variants modulating p53-mediated DNA looping in cancer suppression
proposals.motivation: The p53 tumor suppressor activates gene expression in response to stress by forming long-range DNA loops between its binding sites and target promoters, a mechanism critical for its function. However, current studies rely on a single linear reference genome, ignoring how common structural variants and haplotypes in the human population might alter chromatin architecture and p53 regulatory efficiency. This gap limits our understanding of how natural genetic variation influences a fundamental 3D genome mechanism in cancer predisposition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that common genetic variants, particularly structural variants and SNPs in regulatory regions, create or disrupt chromatin looping anchors, thereby generating 'loop quantitative trait loci (loop-QTLs)' that modulate p53's tumor suppressive network and contribute to cancer risk.
proposals.method: We will first construct a population-based graph genome by integrating common structural variants and haplotypes from the 1000 Genomes Project and gnomAD using the vg toolkit. Using this graph as a reference, we will re-align high-throughput chromatin conformation capture (Hi-C) and p53-specific ChIP-loop data from lymphoblastoid cell lines and primary normal tissues under DNA damage. We will then perform geometric network analysis on the resulting interaction graphs to identify statistically significant p53-mediated loops and correlate their presence and strength with the underlying genetic variation to map loop-QTLs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first map of genetic variants influencing a master regulator's 3D genome architecture, directly linking population genetics to mechanistic cancer biology. It will provide a novel class of functional non-coding variants (loop-QTLs) that may explain individual differences in p53 pathway efficiency and cancer susceptibility, moving beyond static sequence analysis to dynamic structural regulation.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing population-scale graph genomics with the mechanistic dissection of a specific tumor suppressor's 3D action, moving from a single reference framework to a population genetics perspective on DNA looping, a pre-2026 unexplored synthesis.

proposals.title: Mapping p53-Dependent Recruitment to Replication Origins Following Genotoxic Stress In Vivo
proposals.motivation: The p53 tumor suppressor orchestrates the DNA damage response, but its direct role in regulating DNA replication origins remains poorly characterized in vivo. Pre-2026 studies show p53 can bind to replication origins in vitro and influence replication timing, yet a comprehensive, in vivo map of p53 occupancy at origins after damage is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how p53-mediated replication control contributes to genomic stability and tumor suppression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that upon DNA damage, p53 is specifically recruited to a subset of licensed replication origins in vivo, where it modulates local chromatin architecture and origin activity to facilitate DNA repair and suppress mutagenic replication.
proposals.method: We will perform chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) for p53 in non-transformed human cell lines (e.g., RPE-1) before and after treatment with etoposide or ionizing radiation. To identify replication origins, we will perform Repli-seq and/or short nascent strand sequencing (SNS-seq) in parallel. Co-occupancy and network analysis will be conducted by integrating p53 ChIP-seq peaks with origin coordinates using bioinformatics tools like BEDTools and custom R scripts. We will then construct an interaction network linking p53-bound origins to nearby genes and regulatory elements, analyzing topological features (e.g., centrality) for prognostic insights using methods analogous to our prior geometric network analyses in ovarian cancer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first genome-wide, in vivo map of p53 binding to replication origins under genotoxic stress, defining a direct mechanism for p53 in replication fork stability. It will establish a novel network of p53-origin interactions, offering a framework to understand how origin-associated p53 mutations contribute to replication stress and cancer pathogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While p53's role in transcription is well-studied, its direct, in vivo recruitment to replication origins post-damage is a novel and unresolved question. The integration of ChIP-seq with origin-mapping and network analysis to decipher p53's non-transcriptional, replication-centric tumor suppressor function represents a significant conceptual and technical advance.

proposals.title: Biochemical Dissection of Replication Checkpoint Signaling Using a Fully Reconstituted Human Replisome and Synthetic Checkpoint Inputs
proposals.motivation: DNA replication stress and checkpoint activation are fundamental to genomic instability in cancer, yet the precise biochemical mechanism by which checkpoint signals halt the replisome remains unclear. Current cellular and genetic studies are confounded by complex feedback loops and redundancy, preventing isolation of the primary inhibited activity. A minimal, biochemically defined system is needed to map the direct molecular targets of checkpoint kinases within the replication machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core, purified checkpoint signal (e.g., a phospho-mimetic mutant of a key mediator like Claspin or a defined DNA damage structure) will directly and specifically inhibit a discrete biochemical step within the reconstituted replisome, such as CMG helicase translocation or Pol ε/δ polymerase activity.
proposals.method: We will assemble the full human replisome, including CMG helicase, polymerases, clamp loader, and PCNA, on defined, forked DNA templates in vitro using purified components. A synthetic checkpoint signal, such as purified, phospho-mimetic Claspin or a synthetic DNA-protein complex mimicking a stalled fork, will be introduced. Replication activities will be monitored in real-time using single-molecule DNA curtain assays and ensemble biochemical assays (primer extension, helicase unwinding) to pinpoint the blocked step upon signal addition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, causal link between a defined checkpoint signal and a specific biochemical lesion in human DNA replication. It will provide a foundational biochemical framework for understanding replication fidelity and a platform for testing how oncogenic mutations or chemotherapies modulate checkpoint efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While reconstitution of replisome components is established, the integration of a modular, synthetic checkpoint signal as a controlled input to dissect inhibition mechanics in a human system is novel. This synthetic biology approach bypasses cellular complexity to achieve unprecedented mechanistic resolution.

proposals.title: Decoupling p53's Tumor Suppressor Functions: Phage-Displayed Peptide Probes to Isolate Damage-Sensing from Replication-Facilitation Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The p53 tumor suppressor orchestrates diverse cellular responses, including DNA damage repair and replication fork stabilization. However, whether these functions are mediated by distinct structural interfaces on p53 remains unclear, limiting our ability to therapeutically target specific oncogenic p53 mutations. Decoupling these functions is critical for understanding how p53 loss-of-function mutations differentially impact genome stability and cancer progression.
proposals.hypothesis: The p53 DNA-binding domain contains separable structural motifs for binding damaged DNA (damage-sensing) versus undamaged, replication fork-like DNA (replication-facilitation). Peptide mimics of these distinct interfaces can be isolated and used to inhibit one p53 function independently of the other.
proposals.method: We will employ phage-displayed peptide libraries designed around the p53 DNA-binding domain consensus sequence. Parallel biopanning selections will be performed: one against immobilized DNA damage structures (e.g., cisplatin-adducted DNA, dsDNA breaks), and another against undamaged replication fork mimics (e.g., model forked DNA structures). High-throughput sequencing of enriched phage pools will identify peptide families. Binding specificity and affinity will be validated using surface plasmon resonance (SPR). Selected peptides will be fused to cell-penetrating tags (e.g., TAT) for functional testing in p53-proficient and -deficient cell lines, assessing DNA damage response (γH2AX foci) and replication fork progression (DNA fiber assay).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first set of molecular tools to selectively dissect p53's dual roles in DNA damage sensing and replication fork management. It will define the critical amino acid residues that differentiate these interactions, offering new mechanistic insights into p53-mediated genome guardianship. Furthermore, it will establish a proof-of-concept for developing function-specific p53 modulators.
proposals.novelty: While p53's role in damage response is well-studied, its direct function at replication forks is less defined. This approach uniquely uses combinatorial peptide libraries and parallel selection against distinct DNA substrates to functionally deconstruct the p53 protein, moving beyond simple mutation analysis or knockdown studies.

proposals.title: Haplotype-Specific Replication Fork Dynamics Modulated by p53: A Single-Molecule Analysis Using Complete Diploid Genome Templates
proposals.motivation: Replication stress is a major source of genomic instability in cancer, and the tumor suppressor p53 is a key responder. However, existing assays using fragmented DNA or bulk measurements cannot resolve how specific genomic contexts, such as heterozygous loci or complex structural variants, influence replication fork behavior in an allele-specific manner. This gap limits our understanding of how inherited or somatic genetic architecture contributes to replication stress and tumorigenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that p53 modulates replication fork kinetics in a haplotype- and locus-specific manner, and that engineered DNA damage sites will reveal differential fork stalling and restart efficiencies depending on the allelic sequence and local chromatin architecture.
proposals.method: We will generate complete, haplotype-resolved diploid genome assemblies from human cell lines using PacBio HiFi and Hi-C data. Specific genomic regions, including known fragile sites and p53 binding loci, will be synthesized as long (>50kb), haplotype-specific DNA molecules via platforms like Twist Bioscience, incorporating site-specific damage (e.g., AP sites, UV lesions). These molecules will serve as templates for in vitro replication assays using purified human replisome components, with or without p53. Single-molecule replication kinetics will be measured using optical DNA mapping (e.g., NanoChannel arrays) and real-time imaging to track fork progression, stalling, and restart at nucleotide resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first direct, quantitative link between complete diploid genome architecture and single-molecule replication dynamics under p53 regulation. It will provide a foundational map of allele-specific replication stress responses, offering mechanistic insights into how heterozygosity and structural variants influence genome stability. The findings could identify new haplotype-specific vulnerabilities relevant to cancer predisposition and therapy.
proposals.novelty: The integration of complete haplotype-resolved genomes as synthetic replication templates moves beyond fragmented or consensus sequence approaches, enabling the direct interrogation of allele-specific effects. Combining this with single-molecule replication assays and engineered damage creates a controlled system to dissect p53's role at specific genomic loci with unprecedented precision.

proposals.title: A Cascade Screening Strategy Identifies Structurally Stable p53 Gain-of-Function Mutants that Directly Promote DNA Replication Initiation
proposals.motivation: While p53 is a well-characterized tumor suppressor, certain missense mutations confer gain-of-function (GOF) properties that drive cancer progression, metastasis, and chemoresistance. Pre-2026 evidence suggests some GOF mutants can interact with and modulate replication proteins, but a systematic identification of p53 mutants that directly confer a replication-advantage phenotype is lacking. Defining this specific oncogenic mechanism is critical for understanding tumor evolution and designing targeted therapies for cancers harboring these mutants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of p53 DNA-binding domain mutants, beyond simply losing tumor suppressor function, acquire a novel, structurally stable conformation that aberrantly interacts with the replication machinery to stimulate origin firing and fork progression.
proposals.method: We will implement a three-tiered cascade. First, a high-throughput yeast-one-hybrid or modified ChIP-seq assay will screen a p53 mutant library for retention of binding to specific promoter elements (e.g., p21). Second, positive hits will be expressed in p53-null HCT116 cells for orthogonal analysis: DNA fiber combing to measure replication fork dynamics and Repli-seq to map origin firing efficiency. Third, final candidates undergo biophysical analysis (thermal shift assay, limited proteolysis) and immunofluorescence to confirm protein stability and nuclear localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct mechanistic link between specific p53 GOF mutations and the dysregulation of DNA replication, a fundamental cancer hallmark. It will produce a validated catalog of replication-advantage p53 mutants, providing a functional annotation for genomic data. Furthermore, it will identify potential novel therapeutic targets within the mutant p53-replication interactome.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond correlative studies by employing an orthogonal functional cascade to directly connect mutant p53 DNA-binding, biophysical stability, and quantitative replication phenotypes, a strategy not previously applied to this problem.

proposals.title: Mapping the Oncogenic Potential of p53 Mutants Through High-Fidelity Annotation of Non-Canonical Regulatory Elements in the Complete Human Genome
proposals.motivation: The complete, gapless human genome (T2T-CHM13) provides an unprecedented opportunity to identify previously unmapped regulatory elements, particularly in complex, repetitive regions. Many p53 gain-of-function (GOF) mutants drive tumorigenesis through aberrant transcriptional programs, but their binding landscape is poorly defined beyond known canonical sites. This gap limits our understanding of how specific p53 mutants promote oncogenesis by dysregulating replication and other cancer-relevant pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: High-fidelity gene prediction on the complete genome will reveal novel, high-confidence regulatory elements, and specific p53 GOF mutants will bind these elements to activate replication-associated genes, defining a new class of oncogenic function.
proposals.method: We will first apply and benchmark state-of-the-art gene prediction algorithms (e.g., deep learning models like DeepSEA or Basenji2, trained on pre-2026 ENCODE/Roadmap epigenomics data) on the T2T-CHM13 assembly to predict non-coding regulatory elements with high confidence. We will then perform ChIP-seq in isogenic cell lines expressing hotspot p53 GOF mutants (R175H, R273H) and wild-type p53, using antibodies validated for mutant p53. Integration of binding data with our novel regulatory element predictions and transcriptomic (RNA-seq) analysis will identify mutant-specific target genes, with a focus on replication pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a first-of-its-kind map of p53 GOF mutant binding to the newly accessible regions of the complete genome, functionally linking specific mutants to the dysregulation of novel target loci. It will establish a framework for defining oncogenic mutants by their gain-of-access to a previously hidden regulatory landscape, beyond simple loss of wild-type function.
proposals.novelty: The approach synergistically combines the definitive genomic context of the complete human genome with functional validation of p53 mutants, moving the field from cataloging mutations to mechanistically explaining oncogenic GOF through binding to newly annotated, non-canonical regulatory elements.

proposals.title: Functional Dissection of p53-Mediated Tumor Suppression: An In Vivo Toggle System to Isolate the Role of Direct Replication Blockade
proposals.motivation: The p53 tumor suppressor orchestrates a complex network of cellular responses, including cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, and senescence, primarily through its transcriptional activity. However, its direct, transcription-independent interaction with DNA replication factors to block DNA synthesis remains poorly characterized in vivo. Determining the specific, non-redundant contribution of this direct replication block to overall tumor suppression is a critical gap, as it could reveal essential vulnerabilities in cancers that evade p53's transcriptional programs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that p53's direct, physical blockade of DNA replication complexes constitutes a non-redundant tumor-suppressive mechanism, and that selectively disabling this function in an otherwise intact p53 protein will accelerate tumorigenesis in vivo, revealing its specific contribution to the overall tumor suppressor network.
proposals.method: We will engineer a novel knock-in mouse model harboring a point mutation (e.g., R337H or a designed mutation in the DNA-binding domain) that specifically disrupts p53's interaction with the DNA polymerase complex, based on pre-2026 structural data, while preserving its transcriptional activation capability. This 'functional toggle' will be validated in primary MEFs using co-immunoprecipitation and replication assays. We will then employ this model in autochthonous (e.g., Eμ-Myc lymphoma) and transplanted tumor models. Tumor dynamics will be analyzed using geometric network analysis of multiplex immunohistochemistry data from tumor microenvironments to quantify changes in immune cell infiltration and stromal interactions, providing a systems-level view of the perturbation.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the in vivo significance of p53's direct replication blockade, a long-postulated but untested function. It will provide a novel genetic tool for dissecting p53's pleiotropic activities. The findings could identify a specific biochemical interaction as a therapeutic target for cancers resistant to p53-reactivating drugs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting an ecological 'keystone species' isolation strategy to cancer genetics via a precise, single-function p53 mutation, moving beyond complete knockout or broad-domain deletion studies. Combining this with geometric network analysis of the tumor microenvironment represents a unique integration of molecular perturbation and systems-level, spatial profiling.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting the p50 Complex: A Multi-Omic Network Analysis Reveals a Family of Co-Migrating NF-κB Proteins with Distinct Functions in Tumor Immunity
proposals.motivation: The NF-κB transcription factor p50 (NFKB1) is a central regulator of immune response and oncogenesis, but its functional complexity is poorly understood. The canonical view of p50 as a single protein species is challenged by observations of a persistent 54kDa band in immunoblots, suggesting potential co-migrating isoforms or family members with divergent roles in tumor-immune signaling. Resolving this complexity is critical for understanding immune checkpoint inhibitor responses and designing precise cancer therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the immunoblot band historically identified as 'p50' contains multiple distinct protein products of the NFKB1 gene and its interactors, which form unique protein-protein interaction networks that differentially regulate immune gene expression in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged proteomic and network analysis strategy. First, we will use high-resolution mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) with isoelectric focusing to separate and identify proteins within the p50 54kDa band from cancer cell lines. Second, we will use AlphaFold-Multimer to model the 3D structures of identified isoforms and their interactions with known partners like p65 and IκB. Finally, we will apply geometric network analysis to integrate proteomic data with transcriptomic profiles from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) to correlate specific p50 complex members with immune cell infiltration and patient survival outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will redefine the p50 complex as a protein family, providing a detailed map of its constituent members and their interaction networks. It will establish specific isoforms as novel prognostic biomarkers for immune checkpoint therapy in cancers like high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma and identify new, targetable nodes for bispecific antibody or other immunotherapeutic development.
proposals.novelty: While NF-κB biology is well-studied, systematically deconvoluting the p50 band using integrated structural proteomics and geometric network analysis to link specific protein variants to immune phenotypes is a novel approach. This moves beyond the gene-centric view to a functional protein-family perspective in tumor immunology.

proposals.title: Proteomic and Immunogenic Validation of Cell Surface-Localized P50 Isoforms in High-Grade Serous Carcinoma
proposals.motivation: The p53 family member p50 (p53/p47) is a cytoplasmic protein involved in translational control and stress response. Recent evidence suggests p50-reactive molecules are present on the surface of certain cancer cells, potentially serving as neoantigens for immune recognition. Confirming the molecular identity of these surface molecules is critical for understanding their role in tumor immunology and for developing targeted immunotherapies, such as bispecific antibodies, against p50-expressing cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the p50 molecules detected at the cell surface are specific, post-translationally modified isoforms or cleavage products of the canonical cytoplasmic p50 protein, and that their expression correlates with immune cell infiltration and patient prognosis.
proposals.method: We will isolate surface proteins from high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSOC) cell lines and patient-derived organoids via cell-surface biotinylation and streptavidin pulldown, followed by mass spectrometry. Immunoprecipitated surface p50 will be analyzed for unique post-translational modifications (PTMs) and compared to cytoplasmic p50. We will integrate this proteomic data with transcriptomic and clinical outcome data using geometric network analysis to model the relationship between surface p50 isoform expression, tumor immune microenvironment features, and response to immune checkpoint inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether surface p50 is a novel tumor antigen derived from a known cytoplasmic protein. It will provide a mechanistic link between p50 biology and anti-tumor immunity, identifying a potential biomarker for immunotherapy response in HGSOC and a new target for bispecific antibody engineering.
proposals.novelty: While p50's cytoplasmic role is established, its potential cell-surface localization and immunogenic identity are unexplored. Combining cutting-edge surface proteomics with geometric network analysis of tumor-immune interactions represents a novel, integrative approach to validate a cryptic cancer antigen.

proposals.title: Defining the P50-P120 Interaction Network in Abelson Murine Leukemia Virus: Implications for Viral Oncogenesis and Host Cell Signaling
proposals.motivation: The Abelson murine leukemia virus (A-MuLV) is a classic model for studying viral oncogenesis, where the P120 Gag-Abl fusion protein drives transformation. The weak, previously observed association between P120 and the surface glycoprotein P50 suggests a potential regulatory or assembly interaction critical for viral replication or oncogenic signaling. Characterizing this interaction is essential for understanding the full lifecycle of this oncogenic retrovirus and may reveal novel points of vulnerability in viral or oncoprotein networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The weak P50-P120 interaction is a biologically significant association that can be stabilized and characterized using advanced biochemical techniques, and this complex recruits or influences specific host cell signaling proteins to modulate viral infectivity or transformation.
proposals.method: We will employ proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) with P50 as bait in A-MuLV-infected cells to capture weak or transient interactions, followed by streptavidin pull-down and mass spectrometry. Identified interactors, including P120, will be validated using highly sensitive co-immunoprecipitation with crosslinking agents (e.g., DSP) and proximity ligation assay (PLA). The resulting interaction network will be analyzed using established network analysis tools (Cytoscape) to identify key hub proteins and pathways enriched in viral replication and cancer signaling (via DAVID/KEGG).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of the P50 interactome in the context of A-MuLV infection, definitively establishing the nature of its association with the oncogenic P120 protein. It will identify novel host factors co-opted by the virus, potentially revealing new connections between viral surface proteins and intracellular oncogenic signaling pathways.
proposals.novelty: While P120 has been extensively studied, the functional significance of its association with the viral surface protein P50 remains unexplored. Applying modern, sensitive proximity-labeling and network analysis techniques to this classic virological question represents a novel integration of biochemical virology with systems biology approaches to oncogenesis.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Oncogenic Role of P50 in Cellular Transformation through Integrated Network Analysis and Immune Context Profiling
proposals.motivation: The NF-κB transcription factor subunit P50 (NFKB1) is frequently overexpressed in cancers, yet its specific, cell-intrinsic functions beyond canonical NF-κB signaling remain poorly defined. Understanding how elevated P50 levels contribute to transformation is critical, as NF-κB signaling is a known nexus for inflammation, immunity, and cancer, and its dysregulation presents a potential therapeutic vulnerability. This project addresses a key gap in linking a common molecular alteration to specific oncogenic cellular processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increased P50 levels drive cellular transformation by rewiring protein-protein interaction networks and transcriptional programs in a manner distinct from canonical heterodimeric NF-κB signaling, and that this P50-driven state modulates the tumor immune microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics approach. First, we will use AlphaFold-Multimer to model high-confidence P50 homodimer and novel interaction partner structures, guiding proteomic (AP-MS) network analysis in isogenic normal and transformed cell lines. Second, we will perform geometric network analysis (using tools like Cytoscape with network topology metrics) on the resulting P50-centric interactomes to identify key dysregulated modules. Third, we will integrate this with RNA-seq data and leverage pre-2026 immune deconvolution algorithms (e.g., CIBERSORTx) on matched tumor transcriptomics to correlate P50 network states with immune cell infiltration patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a specific, network-based mechanism for P50 in transformation, moving beyond its role as a mere NF-κB component. It will generate a predictive model linking tumor cell-intrinsic P50 activity to immune context, providing a rationale for targeting P50 functions or associated pathways, potentially in combination with immunotherapies like checkpoint inhibitors or bispecific antibodies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying cutting-edge structural prediction (AlphaFold) and geometric network analysis—methods recently used by the PI for MHC and ovarian cancer studies—to deconvolve the unique functions of a single, ubiquitous transcription factor subunit, directly connecting its cell biological role to immune microenvironmental features.

proposals.title: Comparative Phosphoproteomic Mapping of p50 Phosphorylation Sites Reveals Oncogenic Pathway-Specific Signaling Signatures
proposals.motivation: The p50 subunit of NF-κB is a critical transcription factor hub in cancer, with its activity and protein-protein interactions heavily regulated by phosphorylation. Pre-2026 work has established that different oncogenic stimuli (e.g., viral vs. chemical) can activate distinct signaling cascades, but the precise, comparative mapping of resulting phosphorylation sites on a key target like p50 remains incomplete. Defining these site-specific 'phosphorylation barcodes' is essential to understand how divergent oncogenic pathways converge on or differentially modulate NF-κB to drive transformation and influence tumor-immune interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that A-MuLV, SV40, and chemical carcinogens induce transformation via distinct upstream kinases, leading to unique, source-specific patterns of serine phosphorylation on p50 that dictate differential protein interactomes and transcriptional outcomes.
proposals.method: We will immunopurify p50 from isogenic cell lines transformed by A-MuLV, SV40, or methylcholanthrene. Phosphopeptides will be enriched using TiO2 or IMAC columns and analyzed by high-resolution tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Identified phosphorylation sites will be validated by targeted parallel reaction monitoring (PRM). We will then integrate this phosphoproteomic data into a network analysis framework, using tools like Cytoscape, to map kinase-substrate relationships (via NetPhorest/KinasePhos predictions) and model how different phospho-isoforms alter p50's predicted protein interaction network, potentially influencing immune-relevant gene expression programs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative atlas of p50 phosphorylation sites driven by major classes of oncogenic transformation, directly linking etiology to a key regulatory modification. It will identify novel, etiology-specific phosphosites as potential biomarkers or therapeutic targets. Furthermore, by integrating with network analysis, it will generate testable models for how different phospho-isoforms of p50 may rewire cellular signaling and immune gene expression, offering mechanistic insight relevant to immunotherapy response.
proposals.novelty: While p50 phosphorylation is known, a systematic, comparative analysis across transformation sources is lacking. The novelty lies in directly testing the hypothesis that the oncogenic 'insult' writes a specific phospho-code on a common nuclear effector, and in using integrated network analysis to translate discrete biochemical data into systems-level functional predictions relevant to cancer immunobiology.

proposals.title: Systematic mapping of NF-κB p50 subcellular localization and its functional implications across diverse tumor and immune cell types
proposals.motivation: The NF-κB transcription factor p50 (NFKB1) is a central regulator of immune response, inflammation, and cell survival, with complex roles in cancer. Pre-2026 studies, often limited to fibroblasts and lymphoid cells, report inconsistent and indistinct immunofluorescence patterns for p50, suggesting its localization and function may be highly cell-type and context-dependent. A systematic, high-resolution analysis across diverse cell types is critical to resolve these discrepancies and understand how p50's spatial dynamics influence its transcriptional output and role in tumor biology and immunotherapy response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subcellular localization of the NF-κB p50 subunit is not uniform but exhibits distinct, quantifiable patterns across different cell types, and that these patterns correlate with specific transcriptional programs and functional outcomes relevant to tumor-immune interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ high-content, automated immunofluorescence microscopy on a panel of genetically defined human cell lines (epithelial, mesenchymal, and immune lineages) under basal and stimulated conditions (e.g., TNF-α). Using pre-2026 deep learning architectures (specifically, a ResNet-50-based convolutional neural network) for image segmentation and classification, we will quantify p50 localization (nuclear, cytoplasmic, punctate). We will integrate this spatial proteomics data with matched RNA-seq profiles and leverage pre-existing protein-protein interaction networks to model how p50 localization correlates with pathway activity and gene expression modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive, quantitative atlas of p50 subcellular localization across cell types, directly linking spatial information to transcriptional states. It will clarify long-standing ambiguities in the NF-κB field and provide a framework for understanding how p50 localization may influence tumor cell intrinsic properties and responses to immune checkpoint inhibitors, as suggested by recent network analyses in ovarian cancer.
proposals.novelty: While p50's role is well-studied, its systematic spatial proteomics across diverse lineages is lacking. The novelty lies in applying state-of-the-art (pre-2026) geometric deep learning and network analysis to quantify and interpret these indistinct localization patterns, moving beyond qualitative descriptions to generate predictive, cell-type-specific models of p50 function.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Survey of NF-κB p50 Expression and Its Association with Tumor Mutational Landscape and Immune Evasion
proposals.motivation: The NF-κB transcription factor subunit p50 (NFKB1) is a critical regulator of inflammation and immunity, with dual roles in tumorigenesis. Pre-2026 studies suggest p50 can be tumor-suppressive or oncogenic depending on context, but a systematic analysis of its expression across diverse human and animal tumors is lacking. Understanding its pan-cancer expression pattern is crucial for deciphering its role in the tumor-immune microenvironment and its potential as a biomarker or therapeutic target.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that p50 expression levels vary significantly across tumor types and that low p50 expression correlates with specific mutational signatures and an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, influencing response to immune checkpoint inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale computational analysis of p50 (NFKB1) mRNA and protein expression across >10,000 tumors from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and other pre-2026 public repositories. Expression data will be integrated with mutational burden, copy number variation, and immune cell deconvolution (using CIBERSORTx) via a multi-layer network analysis framework. We will specifically model the p50-p65 heterodimer interaction using AlphaFold2 to predict structural consequences of co-occurring mutations in the NF-κB pathway identified in our survey.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish the first comprehensive atlas of p50 expression across cancers, defining tumor subtypes with dysregulated NF-κB signaling. It will identify novel associations between p50 levels, somatic mutations, and immune cell infiltration, providing a rationale for stratifying patients for NF-κB pathway-targeted therapies or immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While NF-κB is well-studied, a systematic pan-cancer survey of the specific p50 subunit is novel. The integration of geometric network analysis of tumor data with AlphaFold2-predicted protein structures to link expression, mutations, and immune context is a unique, hypothesis-generating approach.

proposals.title: A Network-Based Proteomic Screen to Systematically Identify and Validate P50 (NFKB1) Interactors in the Tumor Immune Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: The transcription factor P50 (NFKB1) is a central hub in the NF-κB pathway, critically regulating immune response, inflammation, and cell survival in cancer. While its canonical interactions are known, a comprehensive, unbiased map of its protein-protein interaction (PPI) network in tumor and immune cells is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how P50's diverse functions are modulated in the tumor microenvironment, particularly in the context of immune checkpoint inhibitor response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that P50 engages in a previously uncharacterized set of protein interactions within tumor and tumor-infiltrating immune cells, and that these interactions form functional modules that influence tumor immunogenicity and therapy response.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-phase approach. First, we will perform affinity purification mass spectrometry (AP-MS) using tagged P50 in relevant cancer cell lines and primary immune cells to generate an initial high-confidence interactome. Second, we will integrate this PPI data with existing transcriptomic and proteomic datasets from patient tumors (e.g., TCGA) using geometric network analysis. This computational framework will model the P50 interaction network as a graph, applying community detection algorithms to identify functional modules and centrality measures to prioritize key interactors for validation via co-immunoprecipitation and functional assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, context-specific map of the P50 interactome in cancer-relevant settings. It will identify novel protein partners that may serve as biomarkers for immune checkpoint blockade or reveal new therapeutic targets to modulate the NF-κB pathway. The integrated network analysis will provide a functional model linking specific P50 interactions to patient outcome data.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond the study of P50 alone or its known partners, this proposal applies a systematic, unbiased proteomic screen directly integrated with advanced geometric network analysis—a method recently shown to be prognostic in ovarian cancer—to decode the P50 interaction network's role in the tumor-immune interface.

proposals.title: Evolutionary and Functional Conservation of the p50 (NFKB1) Tumor Suppressor Network from Mouse to Human
proposals.motivation: The murine p50 protein, a component of the NF-κB transcription factor complex, has been implicated in tumor suppression and immune regulation. Understanding its precise, evolutionarily conserved functions in human cells is critical, as NF-κB signaling is a central node in cancer biology, inflammation, and therapy response. Identifying the human homolog and its conserved interaction network will clarify fundamental mechanisms of tumor suppression and may reveal new therapeutic vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: The core tumor-suppressive function of murine p50 is conserved in its human homolog, NFKB1 (p105/p50), and is mediated by a conserved protein-protein interaction network that regulates cell fate decisions in response to oncogenic stress.
proposals.method: We will first use sequence alignment (BLAST, Clustal Omega) and structural prediction (AlphaFold-Multimer) to confirm the human NFKB1 p50 subunit as the direct homolog and model its conserved interfaces. We will then employ integrated network analysis, constructing species-specific protein-protein interaction networks from BioGRID and STRING databases for murine p50 and human NFKB1. Using graph theory algorithms (e.g., for community detection and centrality measures), we will identify a conserved functional module. Functional validation will involve CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of NFKB1 in human cell lines, followed by transcriptomic (RNA-seq) and phenotypic assays for proliferation, apoptosis, and immune pathway activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the conserved p50 network from mouse to human, providing a systems-level view of its tumor-suppressive mechanism. It will generate a validated, cross-species functional model to better interpret murine knockout studies in a human context. The identified conserved network module may serve as a novel biomarker or target set for therapies aimed at modulating NF-κB in cancer.
proposals.novelty: While NFKB1 is known, a systematic, network-based comparison of its conserved functional architecture with murine p50 has not been performed. Integrating pre-2026 AlphaFold for structural homology with geometric network analysis to define a conserved functional module is a novel synthesis of Dr. Levine's recent methodological interests.

ideas.title: Functional validation of p53 mutants via high-fidelity gene prediction in the complete genome
ideas.description: This project leverages the complete human genome sequence as a definitive testbed for advanced gene prediction algorithms to identify and characterize novel, non-canonical regulatory elements. We then screen for p53 mutants that bind these newly predicted, high-confidence regulatory regions, moving beyond known p53 binding sites. The synergy allows us to functionally validate p53 mutants not just for loss of tumor suppression, but specifically for their ability to bind and potentially activate replication-associated genes from these newly annotated loci. This creates a discovery pipeline for oncogenic p53 mutants defined by their gain-of-function interactions with the previously unmapped regulatory landscape of the genome.

Using this graph as a reference, high-throughput assays (e.g., Hi-C, ChIP-loop) will map p53-mediated DNA loops across diverse genomic contexts. This will directly test how natural genetic variation influences DNA looping efficiency between p53-bound damage response elements and specific target sites, potentially identifying regulatory 'loop-QTLs'. The approach moves beyond a single sequence to reveal the population genetics of a fundamental 3D genome regulatory mechanism in cancer suppression.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Trophic Cascade Models to Study p53's Replication Block in Tumor Suppression
ideas.description: The original idea to scale testing of p53's direct replication block to in vivo tumor models parallels the challenge in ecology of isolating the specific impact of a keystone species, like a top predator, on overall ecosystem stability. Ecologists solved this by using controlled mesocosm experiments with selective species removal/reintroduction. Adapting this solution, the research would employ a novel inducible mouse model where a specific p53 mutation (or domain manipulation) allows researchers to selectively disable only its direct replication-blocking interaction with the DNA polymerase complex, while sparing its transcriptional activation and other functions. This 'functional toggle' approach, applied in established autochthonous or transplanted tumor models, would isolate the contribution of this single p53 activity to tumor suppression dynamics in the complex, multi-cellular 'ecosystem' of a living animal, directly testing its non-redundant role.

ideas.title: Phage-Displayed Peptide Probes to Decouple p53's Damage-Sensing from Replication-Facilitation Functions
ideas.description: This project uses phage display to generate peptide libraries mimicking the p53 DNA-binding domain, aiming to isolate distinct peptide subsets. One subset will be selected for binding to damaged DNA structures, capturing the damage-sensing interface, while a parallel selection will use undamaged replication fork-like DNA structures to capture peptides mimicking p53's putative replication-facilitation interface. Comparing these peptide sets will reveal critical structural motifs that differentiate p53's interaction with damaged versus undamaged DNA during replication. Introducing these peptides into cellular models will then allow us to inhibit one function while sparing the other, testing their independent roles in genome stability and replication fidelity.

ideas.title: Haplotype-Resolved Single-Molecule Replication Kinetics of Complete Diploid Genomes
ideas.description: This project combines complete, haplotype-resolved diploid genome assemblies with single-molecule DNA replication assays. We will use the assembled genomes as templates to synthesize long, haplotype-specific DNA molecules containing native sequences and engineered damage sites. These complete, allele-defined molecules will then be used in in vitro replication assays to measure, at the single-molecule level, how the tumor suppressor p53 influences replication fork kinetics. This approach directly links specific genomic architecture and heterozygosity to replication stress responses, uncovering allele- and locus-specific effects impossible to discern in bulk or fragmented DNA studies.

ideas.title: Apply in vitro reconstitution with full replisome and a synthetic checkpoint signal
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology, where minimal, reconstituted systems with defined inputs are used to dissect signaling pathways. The analogous solution involves building a pathway from purified parts on an engineered surface and triggering it with a synthetic signal. Adapted to the original research context, the idea is to reconstitute the full eukaryotic replisome from purified components on a defined DNA template. A key adaptation is the introduction of a synthetic, purified checkpoint signal (e.g., a phospho-mimetic checkpoint protein or a specific DNA structure) as a controlled, modular input. This setup will precisely define the blocked step by observing which replisome activity (unwinding, priming, synthesis, clamp loading) is directly inhibited upon signal addition, isolating the primary biochemical lesion from downstream cellular feedback.

ideas.title: Cascade screening for structurally stable, DNA-binding p53 mutants that confer a replication-advantage phenotype
ideas.description: This redesigned project employs a sequential screening cascade to identify biologically plausible p53 gain-of-replication mutants. The primary screen uses a high-throughput method to select for mutants that maintain specific DNA binding at target promoters. Positives from this screen are then tested in a secondary, orthogonal assay within a p53-null cellular background to rigorously quantify their impact on DNA replication fork dynamics and origin firing. Finally, a tertiary biophysical and cellular localization filter ensures selected mutants are properly folded, stable, and nuclear-localized, guaranteeing that only functionally expressed variants with true replication-stimulatory potential are characterized further.

ideas.title: Cryptographic error-correction of phage-displayed p53 peptide libraries to decode DNA damage recognition codes
ideas.description: This research direction applies error-correcting code algorithms from cryptography to analyze and design phage-displayed peptide libraries that mimic the p53 DNA damage-binding domain. The tandem-repeat-like nature of peptide recognition motifs in the p53-DNA interface is treated as a cryptographic code susceptible to 'sequencing errors' during library screening. By computationally correcting for these biases and redundancies, the method can distill the minimal, high-fidelity interaction codes from noisy screening data. This enables the rational design of next-generation peptide binders that not only mimic but optimally correct and enhance p53's native damage recognition specificity for potential therapeutic or diagnostic use.

ideas.title: Direct inter-domain cooperation within CENP-A nucleosomes decodes centromeric sequence diversity for functional specificity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether the direct, cooperative interaction between the DNA-binding domains of centromere-specific nucleosomes (e.g., within the CENP-A nucleosome core or between CENP-C and CENP-N) is differentially modulated by the diverse DNA sequences underlying different centromeres. It tests the hypothesis that centromeric sequence diversity is not just tolerated but actively sensed, altering the allosteric communication or geometric cooperation between binding domains to recruit a distinct subset of kinetochore proteins. This would establish a direct molecular mechanism by which sequence variation confers functional specificity, determining chromosome-specific assembly, stability, or segregation fidelity. The project moves beyond cataloging sequence differences or protein interactions in isolation to defining a sequence-dependent allosteric code within the centromeric chromatin complex.

ideas.title: Prioritize and Validate High-Confidence Novel Protein-Coding Genes
ideas.description: This redesigned project establishes a systematic pipeline to triage and experimentally validate the most promising candidates from a set of 99 predicted novel protein-coding genes. It first integrates evolutionary, expression, and structural bioinformatics data to generate a confidence score and rank-order the list, focusing subsequent validation efforts on the top-tier candidates. High-priority genes then undergo a staged experimental workflow, beginning with CRISPR-based knockout or knockdown to assess phenotypic impact, followed by protein localization and interaction studies for the most promising hits. This evidence-driven approach ensures efficient use of resources by maximizing the chance of confirming functionally significant genes.

ideas.title: Adapting Broad-Spectrum Antifungal Screening to Test p53's Replication Block Across Diverse Viral Origins
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from antimicrobial drug discovery, specifically the panel-based screening strategy used to identify broad-spectrum antifungal agents. The analogous solution involves testing compounds against a diverse panel of pathogenic fungi to quickly determine the breadth of efficacy. Adapted to the original research seed, this means systematically investigating p53's replication block by testing it not on 'other viral origins' in a one-off manner, but on a defined, diverse panel of DNA viral origins (e.g., from adenovirus, papillomavirus, herpesvirus, baculovirus) in a parallel, standardized assay. This panel-based approach will efficiently determine whether p53's inhibitory function is specific to polyomavirus origins or represents a broader, more general antiviral mechanism against multiple DNA virus families.

ideas.title: Apply ChIP-STOP to map p53 binding dynamics at replication origins after damage
ideas.description: This idea adapts a method from neurobiology, where ChIP is combined with a rapid synaptic tagging protocol (ChIP-STOP) to capture transient transcription factor binding during synaptic plasticity. The analogous solution involves timed crosslinking immediately after neuronal stimulation to 'freeze' brief, activity-dependent interactions. Adapted to the DNA damage context, the research would employ a rapid, timed crosslinking ChIP protocol after genotoxic stress to map the precise, early kinetics of p53 recruitment to cellular replication origins in vivo. This approach aims to resolve the transient, initial DNA binding events of p53 at origins, which likely precede and differ from its more stable binding at gene promoters, providing a dynamic view of its direct role in origin regulation post-damage.

ideas.title: Benchmarking gene prediction against a high-confidence functional atlas to control for assembly and annotation artifacts
ideas.description: This study creates a robust framework for evaluating gene prediction algorithms by first constructing a high-confidence reference set of gene models. This set is built by integrating the new complete genome sequence with orthogonal experimental evidence, such as full-length transcript isoforms and translated protein products, to define regions of unambiguous gene content. The evaluation then proceeds in two tiers: a primary benchmark on these high-confidence regions, and a secondary, exploratory analysis on complex or poorly-supported genomic regions to diagnose algorithmic behavior in the presence of uncertainty. This approach ensures that performance metrics reflect true predictive accuracy rather than artifacts of an imperfect reference.

ideas.title: Single-Molecule Optical Trapping Assay for p53-Modulated Replication Fork Mechanics on Damaged DNA
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core technique from single-molecule biophysics—the optical trapping assay for motor proteins—to the problem of measuring p53's effect on replication of damaged DNA. In the analogous field, optical tweezers measure the force and displacement generated by a single motor protein moving on a cytoskeletal filament to define its mechanochemical kinetics. Here, a single DNA molecule containing a defined lesion (e.g., a cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer) is tethered between two beads held in optical traps, forming a 'DNA dumbbell.' The replication machinery is assembled, and p53 is added in controlled conditions. By monitoring the bead positions with nanometer precision, the assay directly measures the replication fork progression, the force exerted on the template, and the kinetics of pausing or stalling at the damage site. This allows a quantitative, mechanical readout of how p53 modulates the replication kinetics of a single damaged DNA molecule in vitro.

ideas.title: Validate predicted protein-coding genes via a high-throughput functional phenotyping pipeline
ideas.description: This idea adapts the validation pipeline from exoplanet astronomy to experimentally confirm 99 newly predicted protein-coding genes. In astronomy, candidate exoplanets from survey data are prioritized and then confirmed using independent, complementary observational methods. Adapted to genomics, we propose a multi-stage functional pipeline: first, computationally prioritize predictions using integrative scores; second, employ high-throughput CRISPR knockout screens in relevant cell lines to assess phenotypic impact; third, for hits, perform orthogonal validation (e.g., rescue with a wild-type cDNA copy); and finally, conduct biochemical assays on a subset to definitively confirm protein-coding function and characterize their molecular roles. This systematic, tiered approach efficiently allocates resources to move from computational prediction to validated biological function.

ideas.title: Context-Dependent Functional Screening of Synthetic Heterochromatic Sequences In Vivo
ideas.description: This project systematically tests whether sequences destined for heterochromatin assembly possess conditional functions by integrating large libraries of synthetic, randomized DNA segments into various genomic locations in a model organism. The experimental design uses inducible epigenetic modifiers to control heterochromatin formation around the inserts and subjects the engineered organisms to a battery of developmental, environmental, and cellular stress assays. This approach directly tests for function across diverse biological contexts, moving beyond a simple binary search for conserved regulatory motifs. It is designed to reveal whether 'junk' DNA in heterochromatin can play roles in phenotypic robustness, adaptive responses, or genome stability under specific conditions.

ideas.title: Centromeres as Universal Hubs: Testing Functional Equivalence via Epigenetic 'Plug-and-Play'
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that centromeric sequence diversity confers functional specificity by drawing an analogy from network architecture, where diverse hardware functions identically using a universal protocol. The analogous solution is the TCP/IP model, which separates logical function from physical implementation. Adapted to centromere biology, the research would test if function is dictated not by native DNA sequence, but by the ability to establish and maintain the conserved epigenetic mark of CENP-A-containing chromatin. By using genetic engineering to relocate or replace centromeric DNA with divergent sequences and then assaying for proper kinetochore assembly and chromosome segregation, the experiment directly tests if all centromeres are functionally similar when they 'run' the same epigenetic protocol.

ideas.title: Applying Dark Matter Mapping to Junk DNA in Heterochromatin
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that junk DNA in heterochromatin is non-functional by drawing an analogy from cosmology's approach to dark matter. In astrophysics, dark matter's existence and properties are inferred not from direct emission but from its gravitational influence on visible matter and light. Similarly, we propose to search for functional roles within heterochromatic sequences by developing sensitive genomic and biophysical tools to map their indirect, structural effects—such as modulating 3D genome architecture, acting as a sink for protein complexes, or providing mechanical resilience—on the active, gene-rich regions of the genome. This shifts the search from looking for intrinsic regulatory codes within heterochromatin to detecting its extrinsic, constraining influence on the functional genomic ecosystem.

ideas.title: Single-molecule visualization of DNA looping dynamics at acrocentric short arm tandem repeats
ideas.description: This project applies single-molecule imaging techniques, such as DNA curtains and FRET, to directly visualize DNA looping and structural dynamics involving the repetitive sequences on the short arms of human acrocentric chromosomes (p-arms). The goal is to determine if these regions, which contain ribosomal DNA and satellite repeats, form unique, regulated loop architectures that facilitate nucleolar organization, chromosome pairing, or mitotic stability. By observing these events in real time on defined p-arm constructs, we can dissect the kinetics and protein requirements (e.g., CTCF, cohesin, nucleolar proteins) for loop formation and collapse, linking specific structural states to their biological function in ribosomal DNA regulation and genome integrity.

ideas.title: Define the inhibition mechanism using a chromatinized nuclear reconstitution system
ideas.description: This approach employs cell-free Xenopus egg extracts to assemble complete nuclei with chromatin around defined DNA templates, then initiates replication with the full endogenous replisome. The inhibitory agent is introduced to this system, allowing the blocked step to be analyzed within a physiologically relevant nuclear and chromatin context. This captures dependencies on nuclear structure, chromatin modifiers, and DNA topology that a minimal purified system would miss. Subsequent fractionation and add-back experiments can then pinpoint the specific molecular target and step within the established chromatin environment.

ideas.title: Systematically test the functional potential of acrocentric short arm heterochromatin as a model for 'junk' DNA
ideas.description: This project fuses the specific genomic locus of acrocentric short arms with the broad question of heterochromatic 'junk DNA' function. It proposes to leverage newly assembled, gap-free sequences of human acrocentric chromosomes to systematically disrupt, delete, or engineer the repetitive heterochromatic sequences on their short arms. By applying high-throughput genomic editing and functional genomics in cellular models, the research will directly test whether these sequences are essential for nucleolar organization, ribosomal DNA silencing, or chromosome segregation, thereby using a precise, tractable system to resolve the debate on heterochromatic function.

ideas.title: Investigate the acrocentric short arms as a 'nucleolar scaffold' using architectural principles from civil engineering
ideas.description: This research idea applies the problem-solving framework of structural engineering to the biological mystery of acrocentric short arm function. In engineering, the purpose of non-obvious structural elements is revealed by modeling and testing their role in system-wide integrity. The analogous solution is adapted by hypothesizing that the short arms act as a 'nucleolar scaffold.' The research would involve systematically perturbing the short arm architecture—using genomic edits to remove specific repetitive blocks or alter their organization—and then rigorously assessing the functional consequences on the nucleolus, the primary site of ribosome assembly. Key metrics would include nucleolar morphology (via super-resolution imaging), rDNA transcription rates, and chromosome segregation fidelity, particularly under conditions that challenge cellular homeostasis, thereby revealing the structural and regulatory role of these enigmatic genomic regions.

ideas.title: Direct Cooperative DNA Binding via Mechanical Tension and Joint Stabilization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cooperation between DNA binding domains is necessarily indirect, mediated by allosteric changes in the protein or by other bridging proteins. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering, specifically cable-stayed bridge design, where multiple cables directly cooperate through shared mechanical tension to stabilize a structure. The analogous solution involves direct, physical force transmission to a common flexible element. Adapted to molecular biology, the research idea proposes that two DNA binding domains cooperate directly by jointly imposing mechanical strain on the DNA double helix. The binding event of the first domain creates a specific local distortion (e.g., bending, twisting, or compression) in the DNA, which directly alters the geometry and chemical environment to facilitate—or even necessitate—the binding of the second domain to an adjacent site. Their 'cooperation' is thus an emergent property of their combined physical interaction with a deformable substrate, potentially without requiring a change in the protein's conformation (allostery) or a third-party mediator.

ideas.title: Rigorous Energetic Demonstration of Direct Inter-Domain Cooperativity Using Synthetic, Minimally-Allosteric Constructs
ideas.description: This redesigned idea tests for genuine direct cooperativity between DNA-binding domains by eliminating the confounding factor of intra-chain allostery. It uses engineered, physically separate DBD proteins forced into proximity by a synthetic, rigid scaffold that minimizes conformational communication. The key experiment applies single-molecule biophysics to measure the binding energy of this synthetic dimer to a DNA template with two cognate sites. A super-additive binding energy, relative to the unlinked components, provides unambiguous evidence for direct cooperative interplay, such as joint DNA distortion or electrostatic synergy, that is not mediated by the protein's own structural dynamics.

ideas.title: Systematically test the function of heterochromatic 'junk DNA' using a complete, synthetic human Y chromosome assembly
ideas.description: This project leverages a newly generated gapless, complete assembly of the human Y chromosome—a repository of massive heterochromatic blocks—to directly test the functional capacity of its repetitive 'junk DNA'. By using this complete assembly as a blueprint for synthesizing or extensively engineering a Y chromosome in a model system, researchers can perform systematic deletions, rearrangements, and substitutions within heterochromatic regions like the long arm (Yq) and centromere. This allows for unprecedented functional assays of these sequences in chromosome segregation, nuclear organization, and male-specific gene regulation, moving beyond correlation to causation. The result is a definitive, chromosome-scale experiment to determine if constitutive heterochromatin is merely inert packaging or a critical functional component of the human genome.

ideas.title: A Human Y Chromosome Pangenome Resource
ideas.description: This project aims to construct a population-representative, graph-based pangenome for the human Y chromosome to capture its full structural and sequence diversity. It will generate complete, haplotype-resolved Y chromosome assemblies from a globally diverse set of hundreds of male individuals using ultralong-read sequencing technologies. The resulting pangenome graph will explicitly model complex ampliconic regions, palindromes, and large-scale inversions as variation, rather than collapsing them into a single linear sequence. This resource will enable studies of Y chromosome evolution, association of Y-linked variation with traits and diseases, and accurate analysis in genomic medicine beyond the limitations of a single reference.

ideas.title: Dynamic Reference Distillation: Generating Task-Specific Linear References from a Population Graph
ideas.description: This system maintains a canonical population graph as a foundational reference but avoids forcing all tools to use the complex graph directly. Instead, it employs an on-demand distillation engine that analyzes the query data and the analytical task to generate a bespoke, simplified linear reference or a minimal relevant subgraph. This approach ensures computational efficiency and compatibility with existing bioinformatics pipelines while still leveraging the comprehensive genetic diversity encoded in the population graph. The result is a practical, scalable hybrid model that delivers the benefits of graph-based references without their prohibitive computational cost.

ideas.title: Complete the Y chromosome assembly using long-read sequencing and computational scaffolding inspired by cosmic cartography
ideas.description: This research idea aims to generate a gapless, complete assembly for the human Y chromosome by drawing an analogy from cosmic cartography in astronomy. The analogous problem—mapping the universe's large-scale structure from sparse data—was solved by combining deep, multi-source telescopic observations with computational algorithms that used statistical patterns and inference to connect discrete points into continuous structures. Adapted to genomics, the solution involves using multiple ultra-long-read sequencing platforms to achieve deep coverage of the Y chromosome's challenging repetitive and palindromic regions. Then, novel computational scaffolding algorithms, inspired by the techniques used to reconstruct the cosmic web, will be developed. These algorithms will employ pattern recognition, probabilistic linkage models, and Bayesian inference to definitively order and orient the sequencing reads, thereby resolving the final ambiguities and producing the first truly complete, telomere-to-telomere assembly of the human Y chromosome.

ideas.title: A Multi-Reference Atlas for Genomic Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that genomic analysis requires a single, linear reference sequence. Drawing an analogy from geodesy and cartography, where the irregular Earth cannot be perfectly represented on a single flat map, the solution is to adopt a foundational reference frame. Cartography uses a geodetic datum (like WGS84), a mathematical model of the Earth's shape, as a coordinate system for creating various localized map projections. Adapted to genomics, this means building a population-based graph genome that acts as a 'genomic datum'—a multi-dimensional coordinate system of common genetic variation. Analytical tools would then project individual genomes or specific population datasets onto this graph as optimized paths, much like creating a Robinson projection for a continent. This provides a stable, shared framework for comparison while eliminating the bias and loss of information forced by forcing all diversity onto one linear track.

ideas.title: Evolutionary-Informed, Graph-Based Decoding for Tandem Repeat Resolution
ideas.description: This approach combines a probabilistic model of tandem repeat mutation and sequencing with adapted error-correcting decoding. The system first constructs a graph representation of read overlaps within the repetitive region, informed by the evolutionary model's likelihoods for duplication, contraction, and point mutation. An error-correcting code decoder, using soft-input metrics derived from this model, then resolves small-scale errors within repeat units. Finally, a consensus path through the graph is selected by jointly optimizing code parity constraints and the evolutionary plausibility of the inferred repeat structure, directly addressing the context-dependent nature of the problem.

ideas.title: Chromosome-scale haplotype phasing using long-read optical mapping and population scaffolding
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from geophysical seismic tomography to the challenge of creating complete, haplotype-resolved diploid genome assemblies from typical human samples. In the analogous field, sparse direct measurements (boreholes) are combined with abundant indirect seismic data to model large 3D subsurface structures. The adapted idea proposes to generate complete diploid assemblies by first establishing a few definitive, chromosome-scale haplotype anchors—perhaps via ultra-long-range optical mapping or targeted complete sequencing of specific loci. The majority of the genome would then be phased by treating the vast dataset of error-prone long sequencing reads as a population of indirect signals. Computational algorithms would integrate these reads, using population genetic patterns (linkage disequilibrium) and read overlap information to scaffold and assign sequences to the correct parental haplotype, validated and constrained by the direct anchor points. This hybrid approach aims to overcome the limitations of relying solely on either imperfect long-read sequencing or sparse population data.

ideas.title: Apply Reed-Solomon decoding from digital storage to resolve tandem repeats
ideas.description: This idea adapts error-correction techniques from digital storage systems to genome assembly, specifically for resolving tandem repeats. In digital storage (CDs, DVDs, QR codes), Reed-Solomon codes add redundant parity data to blocks, allowing reconstruction of missing/corrupted symbols even when error locations are unknown through algorithms like Berlekamp-Massey. For genome assembly, we treat tandem repeat regions as bursts of ambiguity analogous to communication errors. By encoding flanking unique sequences with synthetic parity information during the assembly graph construction, we can apply Reed-Solomon decoding principles to mathematically resolve the most consistent repeat structure across multiple overlapping reads. This approach uses the redundancy in read coverage to correct for both sequencing errors and assembly collapses in repetitive regions, transforming an ambiguous pattern-matching problem into a constrained error-correction computation.

papers.abstract: The p14ARF tumor suppressor is an important sensor of oncogenic stimuli inducing cell cycle arrest and or apoptosis through both p53-dependent and independent pathways. It is widely demonstrated that ARF prevalent nucleo/nucleolar localization plays an important role in its tumor suppressor function
